title: Indwelling Urinary Catheter: Insertion, Maintenance, and Removal
subtitle: The nurse inserts, maintains, and removes an indwelling urinary catheter safely — sterile insertion, inflating the balloon only after urine return, dependent closed drainage, and early removal to prevent CAUTI.

patient.name: Urinary Catheter Care
patient.admitDate: The nurse inserts, maintains, and removes an indwelling urinary catheter using sterile technique and CAUTI-prevention principles.

labs.v: Front to back; hold labia apart.
Hold penis upright; advance to bifurcation.
Bag below bladder, off the floor.
From the sampling port, not the bag.
Fully deflate balloon; watch first void.

nursesNote: The nurse inserts an indwelling urinary catheter only when clinically indicated, using sterile technique. After positioning the patient and setting up the sterile field, the meatus is cleaned with antiseptic (female: front to back with the labia held apart; male: penis held upright). The catheter is advanced until urine returns and then slightly further before the balloon is inflated with sterile water from the prefilled syringe — never before urine is seen. The catheter is secured to the thigh to prevent traction, the closed drainage bag is hung below the level of the bladder and kept off the floor, and the tubing is kept free of kinks and dependent loops. Daily meatal care is done with soap and water, specimens are drawn from the sampling port (not the bag), and the catheter is removed as early as possible to prevent CAUTI. On removal, the balloon is fully deflated and the nurse monitors for the first spontaneous void within 6-8 hours.
providerOrders: Insert an indwelling urinary catheter using sterile technique for an appropriate indication
Inflate the balloon only after urine return, using the prefilled sterile-water syringe
Maintain a closed system with the bag below bladder level and off the floor
Provide daily meatal care and keep tubing free of kinks/dependent loops
Review daily and remove the catheter as soon as it is no longer needed

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about indwelling catheter care?

opts.text: An indwelling catheter is inserted using sterile technique
The balloon is inflated only after urine return confirms placement
The drainage bag is kept below the level of the bladder
The drainage bag may rest on the floor if it stays below the bladder
The catheter is secured to the thigh to prevent traction on the urethra
A catheter should stay in as long as possible to keep the bed dry

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What guides safe insertion and maintenance?

opts.text: Clean the female meatus front to back while holding the labia apart
Advance the catheter until urine returns, then a bit further before inflating
Maintain a closed drainage system to reduce infection risk
Inflate the balloon with air using any available syringe
Keep the tubing free of kinks and dependent loops
Collect a urine specimen from the sampling port, not the drainage bag

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the steps to insert an indwelling catheter

opts.text: Perform hand hygiene, position the patient, and set up the sterile field
Don sterile gloves and clean the meatus with antiseptic
Insert the catheter until urine returns, then advance slightly
Inflate the balloon with sterile water from the prefilled syringe
Secure the catheter and hang the bag below bladder level
Inflating the balloon before seeing urine return
Keeping the drainage bag below the bladder
Cleaning the female meatus front to back
Letting the drainage spout touch the collection container
Securing the catheter to the patient's thigh
Routinely opening the closed system to irrigate
Using sterile technique for insertion
Performing daily meatal care with soap and water

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Order the steps to remove an indwelling catheter
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Perform hand hygiene and explain the procedure
Attach a syringe and fully deflate the balloon
Gently withdraw the catheter
Provide perineal care and dispose of equipment
Monitor for the first spontaneous void within 6-8 hours
Removing the catheter as soon as it is no longer needed
Keeping the drainage bag on the floor during transport
Maintaining a closed drainage system
Inflating the balloon with the prefilled sterile-water syringe
Routinely disconnecting the bag to measure output
Letting the tubing form a dependent loop below the bag
Hanging the bag below bladder level without kinks
Leaving a catheter in for staff convenience

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Reduction of Risk Potential, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
